title: Designing an Effective Deal Review Process for Complex Negotiations
content:
Traditional deal review systems, commonly known as deal review boards (DRBs), originally evolved to manage capital projects rather than complex commercial negotiations. While a standard capital project can often be approved or rejected based on projected financial risks and returns, a strategic deal requires much more nuance. Negotiators must constantly anticipate what a counterparty may be willing to accept, which inevitably leads to the incremental parceling out of authority. Therefore, a more effective approach for commercial negotiations involves diagnosing perspectives that might be missing from the initial proposal. This proactive evaluation allows teams to explore internal side deals and make necessary enterprise-level trade-offs before finalizing any agreement.

Implementing a successful deal review process requires adhering to several key design principles. Early in the negotiation, it is crucial to manage stakeholder expectations and establish clear operational boundaries. Behind the scenes, team members should rigorously test creative ideas, gather feedback, and continuously adjust their strategies based on new information. Furthermore, negotiators must always be prepared to walk away from the table if the terms become unfavorable. By adopting a dynamic review process that evolves over the life of a deal, organizations can avoid the high failure rates associated with overly rigid, traditional operational mindsets.
